summary: Fowler reflects on the distinctive 'we favor x over y' format used in the Agile Manifesto and explains why it works so well. The key insight is that both sides of each value statement must be genuinely valuable—the format loses power if the unfavored item is obviously bad. The best comparative values force hard choices between desirable things, making them effective for articulating what makes a philosophy distinctive. Fowler also emphasizes brevity as crucial to impact, noting the manifesto's four values and twelve principles kept it punchy. He shares an example of comparative values he wrote to describe Thoughtworks' culture, demonstrating the format's versatility for organizational philosophy.
key_points: The unfavored values in comparative statements must also be valuable—'solving world hunger over slavery' carries no weight because no one supports slavery
A good test is whether someone could reasonably and proudly support the reversed position
Brevity amplifies impact—the manifesto's four values had more punch than forty-six would have
Comparative values highlight contrast between philosophies and force hard choices between desirable things
The format works for organizational values too, not just methodology manifestos
money_quotes: The most important of these is that the unfavored values are valuable too.
The format works when both left and right sides of the 'over' are valuable things that most people want.
The harder choice there is between right and left, the better the value statement.
A good mental test is to imagine someone reversing each value statement, indeed reversing all that you have in the set.
There should be people that you can imagine proudly and reasonably supporting that opposite position.
I'm sure the manifesto would have not had the impact it did if it had forty-six value statements.
This usefulness comes directly from the fact that you are making hard choices between things that are all desirable.
key_insight: Comparative value statements derive their power from forcing genuine trade-offs between desirable things, not from contrasting good with bad.
